citizen_concern.name: Laura M. Kensington
citizen_concern.concern: Persistent potholes and speeding on Maple Ridge Road near Elmwood Elementary
citizen_concern.location_if_applicable: Maple Ridge Road between Oak Street and Pine Avenue, in front of Elmwood Elementary School, Brookfield, MA 01506
citizen_concern.dates_if_applicable: Issue ongoing since approximately 2024-03-01; most recent incidents 2025-11-25 to 2025-12-05
citizen_concern.details:
I am writing to formally express my concern about two related safety issues on Maple Ridge Road, specifically the stretch between Oak Street and Pine Avenue in front of Elmwood Elementary School.

1. Potholes and road surface deterioration:
Over the past year, the condition of this section of Maple Ridge Road has gotten significantly worse. There are currently at least four large potholes and multiple smaller ones in the southbound lane, with the worst one located roughly in front of 214 Maple Ridge Road (the blue two-story house with white shutters). The largest pothole is deep enough that when it fills with water after rain, it is difficult to gauge its depth, and vehicles frequently swerve into the opposite lane to avoid it.

On 2025-11-29, my husband, Daniel, hit this pothole while driving our 2017 Subaru Outback, and it caused a flat tire and a bent rim. We ended up spending $328.47 on repairs at Brookfield Tire & Auto (receipt available if needed). I have personally witnessed at least three other cars pull over with apparent tire damage in the past month, and I’ve seen several near-miss situations where vehicles veered into the oncoming lane to avoid the damaged section of the road, particularly during morning drop-off time for the school.

The road has been patched in previous years, but the repairs seem to be temporary and break down quickly after winter freeze-thaw cycles. Right now, the combination of crumbling asphalt, loose gravel, and uneven patches creates a bumpy and unpredictable driving surface. This is especially concerning given that school buses, parents dropping off kids, and children walking or biking are all using this area every weekday.

2. Speeding in the school zone:
In addition to the potholes, there is a persistent issue with vehicles speeding through this stretch despite the posted 20 mph school zone signs and the flashing lights during drop-off (7:30–8:15 a.m.) and pick-up (2:30–3:15 p.m.). I walk my 9-year-old daughter, Emily, to Elmwood Elementary most mornings, and we have both noticed that many cars appear to be going closer to 35–40 mph, even when the school zone lights are flashing.

On 2025-11-25 at approximately 7:50 a.m., we witnessed a silver pickup truck (I believe a Ford F-150, MA plate starting with "9FJ" but I did not get the full number) drive through the crosswalk at Maple Ridge and Oak Street without slowing down, even though the crossing guard had already stepped out into the crosswalk holding the stop sign. The guard had to physically pull two children back onto the curb. It was a very close call. My daughter was quite shaken by it, and frankly, so was I.

Several neighbors have expressed similar concerns informally. At least three of us (myself, Mark and Jenna from 210 Maple Ridge, and Mrs. Patel from 222 Maple Ridge) have started timing how long it takes for cars to travel between the two school zone signs. Based on our rough calculations using phone timers and distance estimates, a significant number of vehicles are exceeding the 20 mph limit during school hours.

Requested actions:
- Road repair: I would like to request that the town conduct a proper assessment of this section of Maple Ridge Road and schedule more permanent repairs rather than repeated patching. Ideally, this would involve milling and repaving the worst section or at least a more thorough resurfacing, rather than just filling the existing potholes with cold patch.
- Temporary safety measures: Until a full repair can be scheduled, I request that the largest potholes be filled as soon as possible to reduce the immediate risk of accidents, tire damage, and sudden swerving into oncoming traffic.
- Speed enforcement: I also request increased police presence or speed enforcement during school start and end times, even if only on a temporary basis, to deter speeding and make drivers more aware of the school zone.
- Traffic calming: Longer term, I would like the town to consider additional traffic-calming measures in front of Elmwood Elementary, such as raised crosswalks, more visible road markings, or a digital speed feedback sign that shows drivers their current speed compared to the limit.

Personal context:
We have lived at 218 Maple Ridge Road for seven years, and the traffic volume and speed seem to have increased over the last two or three years, likely due to navigation apps routing more drivers through our neighborhood as a shortcut. As a parent of two children who both walk and bike to school (Emily, age 9, and Jacob, age 12, who bikes to the middle school but crosses Maple Ridge), I am very worried that it is only a matter of time before a serious accident occurs if nothing changes.

I am more than willing to provide photos of the potholes, copies of repair receipts, or even short video clips we have taken of cars speeding through the school zone, if that would be helpful. I am also happy to attend a town meeting or speak with someone from Public Works or the Police Department to give additional details.

Thank you for your attention to this matter. I understand that the town has many competing priorities and limited resources, but given that this is directly in front of an elementary school and involves both infrastructure deterioration and speeding, I believe it deserves prompt attention.
